Page 171, here's something kind of interesting. And when it's said, it's like blatantly obvious, but it still says everything. So one piece of information to emerge from the market survey stood out above all others. This is a guy talking about working at Rodale. The guy did our entire approach. The most likely customer for a book on any given topic was someone who had bought a similar book within the preceding 18 months. Well, yeah, of course, right? But what does that really tell us? It tells us that we want to show up and appear as X to an audience that wants X. We don't want to show up to an audience that wants Y and try and convert them. And so when you study all the cult stuff, and I think I put it in here, if I'm not mistaken, yeah, it's at the bottom of page 171. When you study all the cult stuff, cult recruiters do not go out and try and convert people. They go out in search of people who are ready to join a cult. And there's a big difference in those two things. And so now you need to know your profile, and you need to match yourself up with your profile. So there's a whole profile of people who are likely to join cults, and actually a lot of the profile matches most of our customers. So there's some match there. But this is about going where there's a friendly place to go. Going where you're a good match to, not a bad match to. So like, see, I've never wanted to be in the conversion business. So what does that mean? That means I, for the most part, have turned down and avoided speaking engagements where I know I'm going to go into an arena where nobody understands direct marketing and nobody wants to hear about direct marketing, and everybody's going to have their back up about it. I'm not going to do well. Same thing with clients. My whole deal is I've never wanted to go and have to convince the committee. So I'm going to spend a day with a bunch of people who don't get it and try and get them to get it. It's a long, ugly day. I've done it about four times and it ain't no fun. So I'd rather go where they get it. And in the corporate environment, when I have had to go, it's like everybody's got to read this book and everybody's got to listen to this CD and everybody's got to look at this stuff before I show up. Otherwise, I ain't coming. Because starting from zero with any proposition is an ugly thing. So this gets us to source. And source is extremely important. I mean, from a quality of customer standpoint, from an ability to sell to them standpoint, from a retention standpoint, it's a most of the problems in our businesses have as much to do with source as they have to do with anything else. In the get rich in real estate field, you guys don't mind being identified that way. Do you sort of, I mean, well, like you with the mortgage thing. So one of the things you discovered is you got a big problem because you got a lot of broke customers. So as soon as the market goes south, because their financial stability is as thin as a piece of tissue paper, they're all actually really broke. But the guy that owns the shop ain't as broke. And he ain't as thin and he ain't as fragile because he's actually got an office and he's got staff and he's got employees. Yeah, that's right. He can't just walk away. That's right. The other guy can just walk away. But exactly, he's got an office lease. He's got a copy release. He's got, you know, he's, he can't just walk out tomorrow morning and decide, you know, he's going to go sell cars. He doesn't really have that option. So if your entire customer base was made up of them, when the market downturn hit, you would be less affected than you are. If you have a whole bunch of people who can put all their belongings in a knapsack and walk across the street to the car dealership, right? So, yeah. So this, so this now requires thought about who you should be sourcing as a customer, how you should be presenting yourself, what message you should be crafting, what products and services you should be crafting and so forth, what I was about to say about the Get Rich in Real Estate guys. I've lost this battle with everybody, LeGrande and Ondown, but I keep trying to win the battle. So right now, they're in the same shape as your guys are, right? These real estate investors are, any of them that are doing anything, they've got a bunch of properties they can't sell and they can't refinance and they've got negative cash flow, and any of them who weren't doing anything, they're like spent out because they've been at 46 boot camps in the last two months and it's kind of over. And my answer to all of that is, don't bring broke people in the door to start with, right? Source differently. Now that also means you've got to change who you are so you can't be the guy who helps people with no brains, no money, no credit, you know, no contacts, no car and no house to buy real estate with no money down. If that's who you present yourself as, you're going to have rooms full of broke people. And at some point in time, they're going to be too broke to even give you money. But if you've got rooms full of people that got money, then you're in a better position. So this sourcing thing is pretty important. And oftentimes, the taking of the low hanging fruit is like the worst, it's a best short term approach, it's the worst long term approach. So this now is about again, who do you want and then writing to get them. Some of the other stuff I put in here for you, you can go read it at your own pace, but there's some stuff about selective targeting. Page 172 through, I don't know what page, 178, is out of 182. There's some pages here from the Lyman Wood book. And it really tells the story of how he got in the religion business. Do you guys all have his book? Everybody know the book I'm talking about? Okay, there's a book called What A Way to Make a Living by Lyman Wood, you got to have it. And so if you look at page 175, for example, so the basic story here is they were in a business of selling lucky rabbit foot. And they had this great business going selling lucky rabbit feet. And they sent out with the rabbit foot that people bought little pre-done prayers. And eventually the FTC showed up and said, you can't make these claims about rabbit's feet. The FTC however does not regulate religion. And so you can make any claims you want about prayer, you just can't make those claims about rabbit's feet. Now we could have a whole discussion by the way, but we won't. So the solution to their problem, because they had conquered advertising, economics, they had infrastructure, they just had an FTC selling, you can't say that about a rabbit's foot. So the solution was throw the rabbit's foot out and just sell and deliver the prayers. So that's how they got into prayer business. Now there's some very interesting things. If you look at page 176, here's light, here's gold. So we already know our market. Millions and millions of people who want to pray but aren't articulate, they don't know what to say. So we'll publish a magazine as well as prayers and tell them what to say. Huge point here. People want to be told what to do.
